narrative: As an Asian male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model focused on predicting your chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model estimated a 62.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed and avoid high failure risks. This prediction hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, with your undergraduate GPA, Year 3 school decile ranking, and Year 1 school decile ranking being the most influential. Your strong undergrad GPA was the top positive factor, strongly pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar, which makes sense given how it exceeds most applicants' records. However, your Year 3 decile of 1—far below the average of about 5.5—had the next biggest impact, pulling the prediction toward failure by signaling weaker performance in later undergrad years compared to peers. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 1, also much lower than the typical 5.7, reinforced this concern, suggesting early academic struggles that outweighed other strengths like your GPA. Other details, such as your low family income bracket and LSAT score of 22—below the 36.7 average—played supporting roles, but these three stood out as the decision drivers. Compared to typical students, who often rank in mid-deciles and have higher LSATs, your profile showed promise in sustained academic metrics but flagged risks in school-relative performance. Full-time enrollment plans aligned with the norm for 93% of applicants, so that didn't sway things much. While disappointing, this isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male applicant—many with similar backgrounds succeed elsewhere. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking academic advising to improve decile rankings in any ongoing studies, and targeting schools with holistic reviews that weigh your GPA more heavily. Building a stronger narrative around overcoming early challenges could also help in appeals or re-applications. With targeted improvements, your above-average GPA positions you well for future success.
